Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Nineteenth Century

John Stuart Mill

1806 – 1873

Mental chemistry, the permanent possibility of sensation, and other minds as inference.

Mill does not leave us a single, self-contained “philosophy of mind” in the modern sense. What he gives us is a dispersed but remarkably coherent body of doctrine: in A System of Logic, especially Book VI, he defines the science of mind and its methods; in An Examination of Sir William Hamilton’s Philosophy, he analyzes consciousness, perception, matter, the self, other minds, free will, and the possibility of divine or immortal mind; in the Autobiography, he reflects on the education of feeling, imagination, and character; and in Utilitarianism and On Liberty, he applies his psychology to ethics, agency, individuality, and moral development. Mill is best read, therefore, as a philosopher whose philosophy of mind is embedded in logic, epistemology, ethics, and the emerging nineteenth-century science of psychology. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy aptly characterizes him as a nineteenth-century naturalist, utilitarian, liberal, and “thoroughgoing empiricist,” trying to combine Enlightenment empiricism with Romantic and historical currents.

The core reconstruction is this: Mill is an empiricist and associationist about mental formation; a methodological naturalist about psychological science; a phenomenalist about matter; an anti-intuitionist about allegedly self-evident mental truths; a compatibilist about freedom; a non-reductive, provisional pluralist about mind and brain; and, on the self, a thinker who attempts a Humean phenomenal analysis but explicitly acknowledges that memory and personal identity resist full reduction. On the soul, he does not defend incorporeal substance. He treats “spiritual substance” as explanatorily idle. Yet he also does not claim to have refuted God, immortality, or the continuance of a conscious series beyond bodily life. His position is not dogmatic materialism but empirical anti-substantialism.

1. Mill’s starting point: the mind as a subject of science

Mill’s first decisive move is to make mind a legitimate object of empirical science. In Book VI of A System of Logic, he argues that there can be a science of human nature, though it will be an inexact science because individual conduct depends on complex antecedent conditions: motives, character, bodily conditions, education, circumstances, and social environment. He compares the science of mind and conduct to meteorology: lawful, but often too complex for exact prediction in individual cases.

The subject matter of this science is not “mind as thing-in-itself,” but mental phenomena: thoughts, emotions, volitions, sensations, and the laws by which such states succeed one another. Mill explicitly says that psychology must avoid claims about “things in themselves”; it studies the laws of mental phenomena. In a striking formula, he says that “mind means that which feels,” but the scientific study of mind proceeds by examining the succession and coexistence of mental states, not by positing a hidden metaphysical substance behind them.

This is crucial. Mill is not primarily asking, “What immaterial stuff is mind made of?” His question is: what are the observable and inferable laws governing sensations, ideas, beliefs, emotions, desires, volitions, habits, and character? In modern terms, he shifts philosophy of mind away from substance ontology and toward phenomenology, cognitive psychology, developmental explanation, and causal theory.

Yet Mill is not a crude reductionist. He allows that sensations and many mental states have bodily or physiological conditions, and he thinks brain science may someday clarify many mental laws. But he insists that psychology cannot be replaced prematurely by physiology. Even if bodily states underlie mental states, the order of mental succession must still be studied directly. Thus he grants physiology an important future role while preserving psychology as a “distinct and separate science of Mind.”

This gives Mill a layered view of explanation. Mental phenomena may be conditioned by the body, but they also display psychological regularities that must be studied at their own level. This makes him neither a substance dualist nor a reductive physicalist. He is closer to a methodological dual-aspect or dual-level naturalist: bodily science and psychological science are both legitimate, but neither may simply abolish the other.

2. The critique of intuition: consciousness is not an infallible metaphysical oracle

Mill’s philosophy of mind is inseparable from his attack on intuitionism. His central opponent in the Examination is Sir William Hamilton, but the larger target is the Scottish Common Sense tradition and any doctrine claiming that consciousness directly certifies necessary metaphysical truths about matter, self, God, causality, or innate mental principles.

Mill does not deny consciousness. On the contrary, he thinks present consciousness is indubitable in its proper sphere. If I am feeling pain, seeing red, remembering, desiring, or judging, then the occurrence of that present mental state cannot coherently be denied. But Mill sharply distinguishes the fact of consciousness from what philosophers claim consciousness testifies to. Consciousness directly assures me that I am having a sensation or thought; it does not automatically certify that the sensation reveals an external material substance, an incorporeal soul, an innate category, or an unconditioned metaphysical necessity.

This distinction is one of Mill’s most important contributions. He says, in effect, that “consciousness” is often inflated. Philosophers appeal to consciousness not merely for present mental facts, but for alleged truths beyond the present mental occurrence. Mill rejects that inflation. The mind may now find a belief irresistible, but that does not prove the belief original, innate, necessary, or metaphysically guaranteed.

His psychological method is genetic. Before accepting a belief as intuitive, we must ask whether it could have been acquired. Present consciousness is, as he says, overlaid with acquired notions. Associations formed early, repeated constantly, and reinforced socially can become inseparable; once inseparable, they feel immediate. A belief may seem like an original deliverance of consciousness because we no longer remember the process by which it was formed.

This is Mill’s fundamental anti-intuitionist thesis: apparent necessity may be the phenomenological effect of strong association. A belief’s felt inevitability is not proof of its a priori origin. He even argues that the history of science contains repeated cases in which what earlier minds found inconceivable was later rendered conceivable by new evidence, new habits of thought, or new conceptual frameworks.

For philosophy of mind, this means Mill treats the mind as historically and developmentally formed. Mental necessity is often psychological rigidity. Intuition is often sedimented experience. A philosophy of mind must therefore include a psychology of belief-formation.

3. Associationism: the laws of mental formation

Mill inherits associationism from Locke, Hume, Hartley, and especially his father James Mill, but he revises it in important ways. Associationism is the view that many complex mental structures are formed through the lawful connection of simpler mental elements. The SEP describes associationism broadly as the theory that experience sculpts cognitive architecture by linking learning, thought, and mental structure through causal history.

In A System of Logic, Mill identifies basic laws of mental association: impressions give rise to ideas; ideas call up similar ideas; ideas become associated by frequency, contiguity, simultaneity, immediate succession, and intensity. These laws explain how sensations, ideas, emotions, beliefs, and volitions become connected.

But Mill is not a simple atomist. He famously introduces “mental chemistry.” Sometimes complex mental states resemble mechanical aggregates: we can still discern their components. But in other cases, simple ideas or feelings combine into a new mental product whose character is not transparently reducible to its elements. The analogy is chemical combination: hydrogen and oxygen form water, whose properties are not obvious from either element taken alone. Likewise, simple sensations and associations may generate complex beliefs, emotions, sentiments, and volitions with a new qualitative character.

This is an important moderation of older associationism. James Mill’s psychology often seemed to treat mental life as mechanical aggregation. John Stuart Mill preserves the associationist framework but adds emergence. Complex mental life is historically formed from simpler materials, but the formed product may have its own phenomenological and causal character. He also concedes that Hartley and James Mill had explained much, but not everything, and he calls for experimental and comparative methods to test psychological explanations rather than treating association as a universal dogma.

The resulting picture is a proto-naturalistic developmental psychology. Beliefs, desires, sentiments, habits, and purposes are not fixed givens. They are formed by experience, repetition, education, culture, bodily condition, and social arrangement. This doctrine is essential for Mill’s ethics and politics: if minds are formed, then education, liberty, institutions, and culture are not external to psychology; they are part of the causal formation of persons.

4. Consciousness and the unconscious: Mill’s restrictive view

Mill’s view of consciousness is narrower than later psychoanalytic or cognitive views. He resists the idea of “unconscious feelings” or unconscious mental states in the full sense. For Mill, a feeling that is not felt is not a feeling. Where philosophers posit unconscious mental modifications, Mill often prefers to speak of bodily or neural states that may later give rise to conscious states. In his notes to James Mill’s Analysis of the Phenomena of the Human Mind, he argues that hidden links in mental succession need not be unconscious feelings; they may be organic or cerebral states that intervene without themselves being conscious mental phenomena.

This stance has two implications. First, Mill’s psychology remains fundamentally a psychology of consciousness: sensations, ideas, feelings, memories, expectations, volitions, and emotions as experienced. Second, he nevertheless allows subpersonal bodily mediation. He can accept that the nervous system carries processes that affect consciousness without calling those processes “mental” in the same sense as felt experience.

Historically, this places Mill before Freud and before contemporary cognitive science’s expansive notion of unconscious mental representation. His model is still phenomenological in the nineteenth-century sense: the mental is closely tied to what can be felt, remembered, anticipated, or introspectively recognized. But he is not naïve; he sees that physiology may explain gaps in conscious succession.

5. Mind and body: non-reductive naturalism

Mill’s mind-body view is cautious and deliberately anti-dogmatic. Sensations are mental states, even when caused by bodily states. The body may be the antecedent condition of sensation; physiology may uncover the bodily mechanisms; yet the sensation as felt is a state of mind. Mill’s methodological point is that psychology studies the laws according to which one mental state follows another, while physiology studies bodily antecedents and mechanisms.

He explicitly recognizes that differences among persons may arise from original constitution, physical organization, bodily states, or mental history. Bodily causes may act through sensations, appetites, temperaments, or nervous organization. But the explanatory task remains mixed: bodily science cannot simply replace psychological analysis, because the same bodily fact may matter psychologically only through the experience, belief, desire, or habit it produces.

Mill is also alert to the dangers of premature pseudo-science. He thinks brain physiology is advancing and that instincts may be closely tied to brain and nerves, but he rejects phrenology as untenable.

The best description of Mill’s mind-body position is therefore: empirical, naturalistic, non-substantialist, non-reductive, and provisional. He does not infer an immaterial soul from consciousness. But he also does not claim that all psychology has already been reduced to physiology. His position is an early form of layered scientific realism about inquiry, combined with phenomenal restraint about ontology.

6. Perception and matter: permanent possibilities of sensation

Mill’s best-known philosophy-of-mind doctrine is his analysis of matter as “permanent possibilities of sensation.” This is not a mere epistemological aside; it is central to his theory of perception, externality, objecthood, and the relation between mind and world.

Mill begins from the empiricist premise that what is immediately given in perception is sensation. Yet ordinary belief in the external world contains more than momentary sensation. We believe that objects continue to exist when unperceived, that they are public, that they are available to other observers, and that they would produce certain sensations under certain conditions. Mill’s analysis is that belief in matter is belief in a stable order of possible sensations: if I were in such-and-such circumstances, I would have such-and-such sensations.

This is why possible sensations become more “objective” than actual sensations. Actual sensations are private and fleeting. Possible sensations are permanent, law-governed, independent of my present will, and available to others. The table is not reducible to my present visual patch; it is the organized system of possible visual, tactile, and motor sensations that would occur under regular conditions.

Mill argues that this analysis preserves what is essential in ordinary belief in substance. To believe that Calcutta exists when I am not perceiving it is to believe that a vast system of sensations would be available under appropriate conditions, to me or to other possible observers. The “permanence” and “externality” of objects are thus explained by the stability of possibilities, not by positing an unknowable material substratum behind experience.

This is phenomenalism, but not solipsism. Mill does not say the world is a private dream. He says that the content of our belief in matter can be analyzed in terms of permanent, law-governed, intersubjectively available possibilities of sensation. The world’s objectivity consists in its independence from any one actual sensation and its regular availability to possible experience.

This theory stands historically between Berkeley and later phenomenalists. Unlike Berkeley, Mill does not require that objects exist only as ideas in the mind of God. Unlike naïve realism, he denies that consciousness directly certifies material substance. Unlike reductive idealism, he preserves objectivity by appealing to lawful possibility, permanence, and public accessibility.

7. The self: Mill’s attempted phenomenalism and its admitted failure

Mill then asks whether the same analysis can be applied to mind. If matter is a permanent possibility of sensation, can mind be understood as a permanent possibility of feeling? He initially suggests that the mind’s existence during sleep or unconsciousness can be analyzed as the permanent possibility of future conscious states. A mind is not always actually conscious, but it is a continuing capacity or possibility of consciousness.

He goes further: mind might be understood as a series or thread of feelings, together with permanent possibilities of feeling. This sounds close to Hume’s bundle theory. There is no need, on this account, to posit an unknowable mental substance underneath experiences. The self is the ordered continuity of consciousness: sensations, memories, thoughts, emotions, expectations, desires, and volitions.

But Mill then makes one of the most philosophically honest admissions in nineteenth-century philosophy of mind: this analysis does not fully work. The problem is memory and personal identity. A present memory is not merely a present feeling accompanied by an image of a past feeling. It presents the past feeling as mine. The present consciousness refers itself to a past consciousness as belonging to the same continuing subject. The “series of feelings” seems to be aware of itself as a series, including past and future members. That relation of ownership or identity cannot be explained merely by listing feelings in succession.

Mill calls this an intrinsic difficulty. A self understood merely as a series of feelings appears unable to explain how one feeling can apprehend another past feeling as part of the same self. Memory seems to contain an irreducible “tie” binding present consciousness to past consciousness. Mill therefore refuses to pretend that the phenomenal analysis of the self is complete.

At the same time, he does not retreat to substance dualism. He says that whatever the real existence of mind may be, mind is known phenomenally as a series of feelings, and beyond states of consciousness and possibilities of such states, nothing can be asserted about the self with warranted content.

This yields a subtle position. Mill is a phenomenalist about matter, but not a simple bundle theorist about self. He is an agnostic realist about the ego: the self is real, and its reality is disclosed in memory, expectation, and ownership of experience; but we have no positive knowledge of it as an incorporeal substance. The self is not reducible to matter, not reducible without remainder to a mere list of experiences, and not knowable as a metaphysical soul-substance. It is the unresolved center of Mill’s philosophy of mind.

8. Other minds: analogical and inductive knowledge

Mill’s treatment of other minds follows naturally from his theory of self and perception. We do not directly perceive another person’s consciousness. We perceive bodies, expressions, actions, words, and behavioral signs. From our own case, we know a correlation between bodily expressions and inner feelings. We then infer, by analogy and induction, that similar bodies behaving in similar ways are accompanied by similar series of feelings.

This is a classic empiricist solution to the problem of other minds. Mill insists that his phenomenalism does not entail solipsism. Other selves are not unknowable spiritual substances; they are other series of feelings inferred from signs, just as unobserved physical facts are inferred in science. He compares this kind of inference to ordinary scientific reasoning: we infer what is not directly observed from what is observed under stable laws.

The significance is large. Mill relocates the problem of other minds from theology and metaphysics to inductive psychology. I do not need to perceive an incorporeal soul behind your eyes. I need only have good inductive evidence that your bodily and linguistic behavior is connected with feelings, beliefs, and volitions as mine is.

9. The soul, incorporeal mind, God, and immortality

Mill’s view of soul or incorporeal mind is best described as anti-substantialist agnosticism. He does not deny that conscious existence might continue beyond bodily life. He does not deny that a divine mind may exist. But he denies that the hypothesis of an unknowable spiritual substance explains anything about mind better than a phenomenal account in terms of feelings, thoughts, memory, and continuity.

In the Examination, he explicitly argues that the evidence for God, if there is such evidence, is not destroyed by phenomenalism. A divine mind could be conceived as a series of divine thoughts and feelings prolonged through eternity. Similarly, immortality need not mean the persistence of an occult mental substance; it could mean the prolongation of the conscious thread beyond death. The metaphysical theologian loses an a priori argument from “spiritual substance,” but Mill thinks that argument was not worth relying on.

This is not atheistic materialism. Mill says metaphysical skepticism about substance does not logically imply atheism. The design argument, revelation, or other religious arguments would stand or fall on their own evidential merits; they do not depend on proving an immaterial substratum of the self.

The result is a secularized conception of mind. The mind’s reality is not secured by calling it a soul-substance. Its reality is secured by conscious life itself: feeling, memory, expectation, thought, desire, and will. If there is a soul, we do not know it as a metaphysical stuff. We know mind phenomenally, through its states and continuities. If there is immortality, it would be continuity of conscious life, not the survival of an unknowable substance inferred independently of experience.

Historically, this is a decisive transition. Earlier philosophy of mind often treated the soul as the central object: an immaterial, simple, enduring substance. Mill shifts the focus to consciousness, psychological law, personal identity, and the evidential limits of metaphysics. This is part of the nineteenth-century movement from rational psychology to empirical psychology.

10. Will, freedom, motives, and character

Mill’s theory of agency is compatibilist. He accepts that human volitions are causally conditioned by motives, character, dispositions, and circumstances. In A System of Logic, he says that if we knew a person’s character and the motives acting on him fully enough, we could predict his conduct. But he rejects the fatalistic interpretation of this doctrine. Causation does not mean mysterious compulsion. It means regular sequence under conditions.

This distinction lets Mill preserve moral freedom. A person is free when his habits and temptations are not his masters, when he can act on higher desires, purposes, and self-formative motives. Crucially, one’s desire to alter one’s character is itself one of the causes shaping character. We do not stand outside causality; we become agents within causality. Self-cultivation is causally possible because reflective desire, education, new circumstances, and deliberate habits can reshape the mind.

Mill also gives a sophisticated psychology of habit and purpose. Actions may begin as means to pleasure or avoidance of pain, but through association the means may become desired in themselves. Eventually, a person may continue willing an action even when the original pleasure has faded. Purposes and stable habits of willing become central causes of conduct. Character is, in this sense, a formed will.

This theory is essential to Mill’s moral psychology. Freedom is not metaphysical uncaused choice. It is the cultivated power of self-direction within a causal order. The opposite of freedom is not causation but helplessness, compulsion, weakness of character, and inability to act on reflective aims.

11. Ethology: the science of character

Mill wanted psychology to be supplemented by what he called “ethology,” the science of character-formation. Psychology studies the general laws of mind; ethology derives laws of character from those general laws plus circumstances such as education, institutions, social position, culture, and historical environment.

Ethology, for Mill, is not mere introspection and not crude social statistics. It is a deductive and empirical science: from general psychological laws we infer how certain circumstances tend to form certain dispositions, and then we verify those inferences by observation.

This gives Mill’s philosophy of mind an inherently social dimension. Minds are not isolated Cartesian egos. They are formed in families, schools, economies, political institutions, religious cultures, and practices of liberty or conformity. This is why Mill’s politics cannot be separated from his psychology. Liberty matters because individuality, judgment, imagination, and moral courage require conditions favorable to their development.

12. Feeling, imagination, and the correction of narrow Benthamism

Mill’s own mental crisis is philosophically important. In the Autobiography, he says that his early education, shaped by Benthamism and his father’s analytic psychology, had trained him to analyze feelings but not sufficiently to cultivate them. He had accepted that mental and moral feelings are formed by association, and that education should connect pleasure with beneficial things and pain with harmful things. But he discovered that mere analysis can dissolve feelings if they have not been strengthened by a complementary cultivation of emotion and imagination.

After the crisis, Mill concluded that happiness is attained indirectly and that “internal culture” and the cultivation of susceptibilities are essential. He came to see poetry, especially Wordsworth, not as decorative illusion but as a medicine for the feelings and a means of emotional education.

This modifies his philosophy of mind. He remains an associationist, but he no longer treats the mind as a cold calculus of ideas and pleasures. Human beings need affective depth, imagination, sympathy, dignity, and individuality. In Utilitarianism, this appears in his doctrine of higher pleasures: pleasures of intellect, feeling, imagination, and moral sentiment are qualitatively superior to mere sensation, and the competent judges are those who know both kinds.

Thus Mill’s mature psychology is not Benthamite hedonistic atomism. It is a developmental psychology of higher faculties. The mind is educable upward or downward. It can lose noble capacities through neglect, conformity, or bad institutions; it can also be cultivated through art, liberty, sympathy, and reflective self-formation.

13. Logic, psychology, and “psychologism”

Mill’s philosophy of mind also shaped his logic. He holds that logic is an art of reasoning, but the theoretical basis of that art is connected to psychology: to understand rules of reasoning, we need to understand the mental processes of inference, belief, evidence, and error. The SEP’s article on psychologism notes that Mill treats psychology as providing the theoretical grounds for logic in this respect, while also recognizing that this creates interpretive tensions.

This is one reason Mill became historically important in the later anti-psychologism debates. Frege and Husserl would reject the idea that logic is grounded in psychological laws, insisting that logical validity is not a fact about how humans happen to think. Husserl’s critique of psychologism in the Logical Investigations became a key text in the early twentieth-century debate.

For philosophy of mind, the point is that Mill belongs to a moment before the modern separation between psychology, logic, epistemology, and philosophy of mind. In his framework, the study of thought as a psychological process and the study of thought as a norm-governed activity are still deeply entangled. That entanglement is both a strength and a vulnerability. It lets Mill naturalize reason, but it also raises the problem of whether norms of validity can be reduced to facts about mental association.

14. Historical position in the development of philosophy of mind

Mill occupies a pivotal position between early modern empiricism and later scientific psychology. He inherits from Locke and Hume the thesis that knowledge must be traced to experience; from Hartley and James Mill the associationist program; from Bentham the project of applying psychology to ethics and social reform; and from the Scottish and Kantian debates a set of opponents who defend intuition, innate necessity, or direct realism. Associationism in this period was not merely a psychological doctrine but a general empiricist strategy for explaining learning, thought, mental structure, and belief-formation.

The Examination of Sir William Hamilton’s Philosophy is especially important because it comes at a time when there was not yet a sharp boundary between empirical psychology and philosophy of mind. Alan Ryan’s introduction to the Online Library of Liberty edition emphasizes that nineteenth-century disputes about consciousness, Kant, Hamilton, and Mill did not operate with the later division between philosophical analysis and empirical psychology.

Mill also stands just before the institutional rise of experimental psychology. Wundt, later known as a founder of experimental psychology, established the first psychology laboratory at Leipzig in the later nineteenth century; Britannica dates this to 1879, six years after Mill’s death. The SEP likewise describes Wundt as the “father of experimental psychology” and founder of the first psychology laboratory. Mill’s psychology is therefore pre-laboratory but post-metaphysical: it tries to make mind scientific before psychology becomes a fully institutional experimental discipline.

In the longer philosophy-of-mind trajectory, Mill matters for at least six reasons.

First, his phenomenalism about matter anticipates later attempts to analyze physical objects as logical or experiential constructions.

Second, his problem of the self anticipates later debates over bundle theory, personal identity, memory, and the irreducibility of first-person ownership.

Third, his other-minds argument gives a classic empiricist model of analogical inference from behavior to mentality.

Fourth, his mental chemistry anticipates emergentist themes: complex mental states may arise from simpler elements without being mere sums.

Fifth, his distinction between psychology and physiology anticipates later debates over levels of explanation in cognitive science and neuroscience.

Sixth, his psychologism about logic helped provoke the later anti-psychologistic turn in analytic philosophy and phenomenology.

15. The extracted Millian philosophy of mind

A comprehensive Millian philosophy of mind can be stated in the following theses.

The mind is known phenomenally, not noumenally. We know feelings, sensations, thoughts, emotions, volitions, memories, and expectations. We do not know an underlying mental substance as an object of direct cognition. “Mind” is that which feels, but science studies the laws of the feelings and their succession, not an occult substrate.

Consciousness is authoritative only within limits. It certifies present mental occurrence, not the metaphysical interpretation of that occurrence. A present belief may feel necessary without being innate or a priori.

Mental life is formed by association. Ideas, emotions, beliefs, motives, and habits become connected through repetition, contiguity, resemblance, intensity, and education. What seems immediate may be acquired.

Complex mental states may be chemically, not merely mechanically, composed. Higher sentiments, moral feelings, purposes, and intellectual capacities may emerge from simpler materials but possess new qualities as organized wholes.

Matter is not known as an unknowable substance but as a system of permanent possibilities of sensation. Externality, objectivity, and permanence are explained by law-governed possible experience, public accessibility, and independence from momentary perception.

The self is not adequately explained as material substance or spiritual substance. Mill attempts to analyze it as a series of feelings plus possibilities, but memory reveals an irreducible relation of identity: present consciousness refers to past consciousness as its own. The ego is therefore phenomenally known but metaphysically obscure.

Other minds are known inductively. We infer other series of feelings from bodies, behavior, language, and analogy with our own case. This inference is fallible but rational and continuous with ordinary scientific inference.

Mind and body are connected, but psychology remains autonomous until physiology can actually explain its phenomena. Bodily states condition mental states, but mental laws must be studied at the psychological level.

Freedom is self-formation under causality. Human action is caused by motives and character, but character can be modified by desire, habit, education, and deliberate placement of oneself under new circumstances. Freedom is not uncaused willing; it is cultivated self-command.

The soul as incorporeal substance is epistemically idle. Mill does not need it to explain mind. But he does not claim that phenomenalism disproves God or immortality. A divine or immortal mind could be conceived as a continuing series or thread of consciousness.

The mind is ethically and socially educable. Feelings, imagination, sympathy, dignity, and higher pleasures must be cultivated. A psychology of mind is incomplete unless it includes character, culture, liberty, and the conditions under which higher faculties flourish.

16. Critical evaluation

Mill’s philosophy of mind has major strengths. It is disciplined about evidence. It refuses to smuggle metaphysics into “consciousness.” It explains why acquired beliefs can feel necessary. It respects the reality of conscious life without invoking an unknowable soul-substance. It recognizes both psychological and physiological explanation. It gives a powerful developmental account of character, agency, and moral education. And, in the self problem, it is unusually honest: Mill sees that a pure bundle theory cannot easily account for memory and first-person identity.

Its weaknesses are also clear. Mill’s associationism is too narrow to serve as a complete psychology by contemporary standards. His restrictive view of unconscious mentality underestimates the possibility of unconscious representation, implicit cognition, and nonconscious affective processing. His phenomenalism about matter has difficulty explaining scientific realism about unobservable entities. His account of the self identifies a real problem but does not solve it. His psychologism about logic exposes him to the later Fregean and Husserlian charge that laws of valid inference cannot be reduced to facts about how minds associate ideas.

Still, Mill’s importance is not diminished by these limits. He stands at a crucial historical junction: after the metaphysics of soul-substance had lost much of its explanatory force, but before experimental psychology and neuroscience had become dominant. He tries to construct a philosophy of mind that is empiricist without being eliminativist, naturalistic without being crudely materialist, phenomenalist without collapsing into solipsism, and developmental without denying agency. His deepest unresolved problem—the self as more than a series yet not knowable as substance—remains one of the central problems of philosophy of mind.
